ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class (FOODON_03302757); no specific vegetarian-filled steamed bun subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers steamed bread and buns as a category; no specific vegetarian-filled bun code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for steamed vegetarian bun. SNOMED CT does not have sufficient granularity for this prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One steamed vegetarian bun (approximately 80 g) is a typical single-piece serving as served at dim sum restaurants such as Din Tai Fung in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Bun with Vegetable Filling — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for steamed vegetable buns
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO-INFOODS / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exactly matches Din Tai Fung's proprietary recipe. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for steamed buns with vegetable filling (FDC ID 169752 and related entries) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for steamed vegetable buns. Per-100g values reflect a typical steamed vegetarian bun with wheat-flour dough and mixed vegetable-tofu-vermicelli filling. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of steamed wheat-flour buns and analogous dim sum items — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed vegetarian bun (素菜包) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from the closest analogous food: steamed wheat-flour buns (baozi) with vegetable filling, which are reported in the range GI 40–50 in the University of Sydney GI Database and in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care. The vegetable-tofu-vermicelli filling lowers the overall GI relative to plain steamed buns (GI ~55). GL per serving calculated as (45 × 30.0 g available carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 13.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~248 mg per 80 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Each steamed vegetarian bun contributes approximately 248 mg sodium per piece; individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one piece per meal and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or chilli sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~13.5 per serving) from refined wheat-flour dough
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of 13.5 per bun, steamed vegetarian buns have a moderate impact on postprandial blood glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece per sitting and pair with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and zero cholesterol from plant-based filling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The vegetarian filling (Nai Bai, mushrooms, tofu, vermicelli) contributes negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol per serving, making steamed vegetarian buns a relatively heart-friendly dim sum choice, though sodium intake should still be monitored in the context of a cardiovascular-protective diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Vegetarian Bun (素菜包, Sù cài bāo) is a classic Chinese dim sum item consisting of a soft, fluffy, leavened wheat-flour dough wrapper encasing a savoury vegetarian filling. At Din Tai Fung in Singapore, the filling is composed of Nai Bai (a variety of Chinese cabbage), shiitake mushrooms, glass vermicelli (mung bean or sweet potato starch noodles), and firm tofu (beancurd) cubes, seasoned with sesame oil and soy sauce. The bun is prepared by steaming, which produces a moist, pillowy exterior with a tender, slightly chewy bite. The filling is mildly savoury with earthy mushroom notes and a delicate sweetness from the Nai Bai. Each bun weighs approximately 80 g and provides around 158 kcal, 5 g protein, 2.2 g fat, and 30 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low GI), making it a moderate glycemic-load food per serving (GL ~13.5). Sodium content is moderate at approximately 248 mg per piece, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is suitable for vegetarians and is halal-friendly. It is free from cholesterol and low in saturated fat due to its plant-based filling. Clinically, it is a reasonable dim sum choice for individuals with cardiovascular disease but should be consumed in moderation by those with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its refined carbohydrate content and sodium level. The dish is widely available at Chinese restaurants and dim sum establishments across Singapore and is a staple of Cantonese and Shanghainese dim sum menus.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for Din Tai Fung's proprietary steamed vegetarian bun exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central steamed vegetable bun entries and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for comparable steamed vegetarian buns. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for steamed wheat-flour buns with vegetable filling. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food type is well-characterised in approved sources and the adaptation is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of steamed vegetarian bun as prepared (dough + filling combined). Serving size of 80 g (1 piece) is an estimate based on typical dim sum bun weights; Din Tai Fung's actual piece weight may vary. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a fully plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plant-based steamed buns. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the food contains no significant vitamin D sources (no animal fat, no fortified ingredients); confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the bun is steamed with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as all filling ingredients are plant-based; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 0.9 g from sesame oil and soy components). 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific product has not been published. 8. The cuisine_tag 'Noodles' in the input is not reflected in the food_group as the primary identity of this food is a steamed bun (grains and staples), not a noodle dish. 9. is_vegan set to false because the dough may contain trace dairy or egg in some restaurant preparations; Din Tai Fung's specific formulation is not publicly confirmed as vegan. 10. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated or confirmed numeric values where defensible sources exist; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
